name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: DNS simulation — 2D Navier-Stokes with inverse cascade, tracer advection, and data output
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Implement GPU-accelerated pseudo-spectral 2D NS solver in vorticity-streamfunction form using PyTorch (device='cuda') with torch.fft.rfft2/irfft2
        - Use N=512 grid, domain [0,2π]², forcing at k∈[10,12], hyperviscosity ν_h=1e-28, p=4, integrating factor method for hyperviscous term (mandatory)
        - Calibrate force_amp empirically: inject with force_amp=1 for 20 steps at dt=0.1, measure dE/dt per unit amp², set force_amp=sqrt(epsilon_inj/dE_per_amp2); expected ~80,000–100,000
        - Spinup: run until T_spinup=20.0, monitoring k_peak; save diagnostics only during spinup
        - At end of spinup, compute T_L = L/U_rms, seed N_tracers=5000 uniformly, begin production run
        - Production: run for T_prod=50×T_L, saving tracer positions+velocities at dt_snap=0.01, Eulerian velocity/vorticity coarsened 512→128 at dt_vel=0.5
        - Save all output files to /home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/data/ with exact shapes and dtypes specified in the problem statement (adapted for N=512, 128² coarsened fields)
        - Save sim_params.json with all key parameters
        - Verify before production: U_rms*dt_snap < 0.1, T_L finite and >0, k_peak ≥ 2
        - Print key diagnostics at regular intervals during run
      * code_execution_timeout: 7200

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: Data quality verification and inverse cascade validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy from the data directory
        - Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot ⟨|Δr(1 step)|⟩ accounting for periodic boundary conditions; verify it is < 0.3×2π ≈ 1.88; print result and flag if exceeded
        - Compute VACF C_vv(τ) at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; verify C_vv(τ=1) > 0.3; report decorrelation time τ_corr (first lag where C_vv drops below 1/e)
        - Confirm inverse cascade: extract E(k,t) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod from energy_spectrum.npy; identify k_peak at each time; verify drift from ~10–12 toward lower k
        - Generate a single figure with 3 subplots: (1) mean displacement vs time, (2) VACF vs lag, (3) E(k) at 4 time fractions showing cascade evolution
        - Print all key numerical results (mean displacement, C_vv values, k_peak evolution)

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: Non-stationarity analysis — α(t), γ(t), ν(t), k_peak(t) across overlapping time windows
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy
        - Define overlapping windows of width W=8 T_L, step ΔW=2 T_L; compute number of windows (~26)
        - For each window: compute displacement PDFs at lags τ={0.5,1,2}×T_L; fit α via McCulloch quantile method (ν_α=(q95-q5)/(q75-q25), interpolate from McCulloch 1986 table); also compute Hill estimator as cross-check
        - For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law to get γ(t); fit VACF algebraic decay to get ν(t); extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics
        - Check CCDF log-log linearity (≥1.5 decades); flag non-power-law windows
        - Perform ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on α(t) time series
        - Fit α = a + b×(k_peak/k_box)^c
        - Save all window results (α, γ, ν, d_vv, k_peak, window center times) to a numpy/npz file for use in later steps

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Plot non-stationarity analysis results
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load saved window results from Step 3
        - Generate a multi-panel figure showing: α(t), γ(t), ν(t) vs time; k_peak(t) vs time; d_vv(t) vs time; α vs k_peak/k_box with fitted curve α=a+b×(k_peak/k_box)^c
        - Mark flagged non-power-law windows differently (e.g., open symbols)
        - Include ADF/KPSS test results as text annotations on the α(t) panel
        - Print all fitted parameters and stationarity test statistics

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Wavelet decomposition, eddy-lifetime estimation, and filtered tracer re-advection
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape: N_vel_snaps × 2 × 128 × 128) and tracer_positions.npy
        - Apply 2D DWT using db4 wavelet (periodic boundary mode) to each velocity snapshot at levels j=1..5; extract detail coefficients D_j^{H,V,D} at each level
        - Reconstruct filtered velocity fields ũ_j for j_min∈{1,2,3,4} by zeroing detail coefficients below j_min; save reconstructed fields
        - Compute wavelet energy E_j(x,y,t) = Σ_d |D_j^d(x,y,t)|² for each level j and snapshot t
        - Compute temporal autocorrelation C_E(τ,j) = ⟨E_j(t)E_j(t+τ)⟩/⟨E_j²⟩ averaged over spatial positions; integrate until C_E < 0.1 to get T_eddy(j)
        - For each filtered field ũ_j: re-advect 5000 tracers (seeded at same initial positions as DNS tracers) using RK4 integration with bilinear interpolation of filtered velocity; use GPU (device=cuda) for interpolation
        - Compute α(j) via McCulloch method, γ_MSD(j) from MSD power law, ν_VACF(j) from VACF decay for each filtered field
        - Save all wavelet results (T_eddy per level, α(j), γ(j), ν(j), filtered field statistics) to npz file
      * code_execution_timeout: 7200

- Step 6:
      * sub-task: Plot wavelet scaling results and CTRW prediction test
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load wavelet results from Step 5
        - Generate multi-panel figure: (1) α(j) vs T_eddy(j) with fitted curve and CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β); (2) γ_MSD(j) vs j; (3) ν_VACF(j) vs j; (4) T_eddy(j) vs wavelet level j
        - Fit β(j) ∝ T_eddy(j)^δ and report δ; compute predicted α from CTRW formula and overlay on plot
        - Print all fitted parameters (δ, functional form of α vs T_eddy)

- Step 7:
      * sub-task: Synthesis — compare α(t) vs α(j), 2D map, stationarity conclusion
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load window results from Step 3 and wavelet results from Step 5
        - For each time window, identify dominant wavelet scale j_peak(t) as the level j whose characteristic wavenumber best matches k_peak(t) (k_j ≈ 128/2^j for 128² fields)
        - Compare α(t) from Task 2 with α(j_peak(t)) from Task 3 on a single plot; compute correlation coefficient
        - Construct 2D scatter/interpolated map of α as function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using all available (window, wavelet-level) data points
        - Save synthesis results to npz file

- Step 8:
      * sub-task: Plot synthesis results
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load synthesis results from Step 7
        - Generate figure with: (1) α(t) vs α(j_peak(t)) comparison panel; (2) 2D map of α(T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) as scatter or interpolated contour plot
        - Print correlation statistics and key synthesis numbers

- Step 9:
      * sub-task: Write full Results section of the paper
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 2–8: displacement quality metrics, VACF decorrelation, inverse cascade k_peak evolution, α(t)/γ(t)/ν(t) time series, stationarity test outcomes, wavelet T_eddy(j) values, α(j) scaling, CTRW prediction test, synthesis comparison
        - Report all fitted parameters with uncertainties, test statistics, and physical interpretations
        - Discuss whether α≈1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts with spectral condensation
        - Discuss whether CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β) with β∝T_eddy^δ is supported
        - Reference all generated plots by description
        - Write in academic paper Results section style with subsections matching the four analysis tasks

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:

		- Calibrate force_amp empirically: inject with force_amp=1 for 20 steps at dt=0.1, measure dE/dt per unit amp², set force_amp=sqrt(epsilon_inj/dE_per_amp2); expected ~80,000–100,000
		- Spinup: run until T_spinup=20.0, monitoring k_peak; save diagnostics only during spinup
		- At end of spinup, compute T_L = L/U_rms, seed N_tracers=5000 uniformly, begin production run
		- Production: run for T_prod=50×T_L, saving tracer positions+velocities at dt_snap=0.01, Eulerian velocity/vorticity coarsened 512→128 at dt_vel=0.5
		- Save all output files to /home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/data/ with exact shapes and dtypes specified in the problem statement (adapted for N=512, 128² coarsened fields)
		- Save sim_params.json with all key parameters
		- Verify before production: U_rms*dt_snap < 0.1, T_L finite and >0, k_peak ≥ 2

		- Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot ⟨|Δr(1 step)|⟩ accounting for periodic boundary conditions; verify it is < 0.3×2π ≈ 1.88; print result and flag if exceeded
		- Compute VACF C_vv(τ) at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; verify C_vv(τ=1) > 0.3; report decorrelation time τ_corr (first lag where C_vv drops below 1/e)
		- Confirm inverse cascade: extract E(k,t) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod from energy_spectrum.npy; identify k_peak at each time; verify drift from ~10–12 toward lower k
		- Generate a single figure with 3 subplots: (1) mean displacement vs time, (2) VACF vs lag, (3) E(k) at 4 time fractions showing cascade evolution
		- Print all key numerical results (mean displacement, C_vv values, k_peak evolution)

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Non-stationarity analysis — α(t), γ(t), ν(t), k_peak(t) across overlapping time windows
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy
		- Define overlapping windows of width W=8 T_L, step ΔW=2 T_L; compute number of windows (~26)
		- For each window: compute displacement PDFs at lags τ={0.5,1,2}×T_L; fit α via McCulloch quantile method (ν_α=(q95-q5)/(q75-q25), interpolate from McCulloch 1986 table); also compute Hill estimator as cross-check
		- For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law to get γ(t); fit VACF algebraic decay to get ν(t); extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics
		- Check CCDF log-log linearity (≥1.5 decades); flag non-power-law windows
		- Perform ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on α(t) time series
		- Fit α = a + b×(k_peak/k_box)^c
		- Save all window results (α, γ, ν, d_vv, k_peak, window center times) to a numpy/npz file for use in later steps

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Plot non-stationarity analysis results
	* agent in charge: engineer

		- Load saved window results from Step 3
		- Generate a multi-panel figure showing: α(t), γ(t), ν(t) vs time; k_peak(t) vs time; d_vv(t) vs time; α vs k_peak/k_box with fitted curve α=a+b×(k_peak/k_box)^c
		- Mark flagged non-power-law windows differently (e.g., open symbols)
		- Include ADF/KPSS test results as text annotations on the α(t) panel
		- Print all fitted parameters and stationarity test statistics

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Wavelet decomposition, eddy-lifetime estimation, and filtered tracer re-advection
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 7200s

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape: N_vel_snaps × 2 × 128 × 128) and tracer_positions.npy
		- Apply 2D DWT using db4 wavelet (periodic boundary mode) to each velocity snapshot at levels j=1..5; extract detail coefficients D_j^{H,V,D} at each level
		- Reconstruct filtered velocity fields ũ_j for j_min∈{1,2,3,4} by zeroing detail coefficients below j_min; save reconstructed fields
		- Compute wavelet energy E_j(x,y,t) = Σ_d |D_j^d(x,y,t)|² for each level j and snapshot t
		- Compute temporal autocorrelation C_E(τ,j) = ⟨E_j(t)E_j(t+τ)⟩/⟨E_j²⟩ averaged over spatial positions; integrate until C_E < 0.1 to get T_eddy(j)
		- For each filtered field ũ_j: re-advect 5000 tracers (seeded at same initial positions as DNS tracers) using RK4 integration with bilinear interpolation of filtered velocity; use GPU (device=cuda) for interpolation
		- Compute α(j) via McCulloch method, γ_MSD(j) from MSD power law, ν_VACF(j) from VACF decay for each filtered field
		- Save all wavelet results (T_eddy per level, α(j), γ(j), ν(j), filtered field statistics) to npz file

		- Fit β(j) ∝ T_eddy(j)^δ and report δ; compute predicted α from CTRW formula and overlay on plot

		- For each time window, identify dominant wavelet scale j_peak(t) as the level j whose characteristic wavenumber best matches k_peak(t) (k_j ≈ 128/2^j for 128² fields)
		- Compare α(t) from Task 2 with α(j_peak(t)) from Task 3 on a single plot; compute correlation coefficient
		- Construct 2D scatter/interpolated map of α as function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using all available (window, wavelet-level) data points

		- Report all fitted parameters with uncertainties, test statistics, and physical interpretations
		- Discuss whether α≈1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts with spectral condensation
		- Discuss whether CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β) with β∝T_eddy^δ is supported
		- Reference all generated plots by description
		- Write in academic paper Results section style with subsections matching the four analysis tasks

name: plan_reviewer
content:
**Recommendations:**

- Step 1 (DNS simulation): The instruction to coarsen velocity/vorticity fields from 512→128 during the simulation conflicts with the problem statement's mandatory output shapes specifying 256×256 coarsened fields. The engineer should be explicitly instructed to coarsen to 128×128 (consistent with the N=512 plan deviation) AND to document this discrepancy from the problem statement in sim_params.json, so that downstream steps (especially Step 5 wavelet decomposition) load the correct 128×128 fields rather than expecting 256×256.

- Step 1 (DNS simulation): The instruction to save energy_spectrum.npy with shape (N_tracer_snaps, N//2) is ambiguous when N=512 — this means 256 wavenumber bins. The engineer should be told to save the azimuthally-averaged spectrum at every tracer snapshot time (not just at Eulerian snapshot times), and to explicitly print the shape of energy_spectrum.npy after saving so downstream steps can verify the array dimensions before loading.

- Step 1 (DNS simulation): The production run duration T_prod = 50×T_L could result in an extremely large number of tracer snapshots if T_L is large (e.g., T_L=10 → T_prod=500 → 50,000 snapshots at dt_snap=0.01). The engineer should be instructed to cap N_tracer_snaps at a maximum of 10,000 by adaptively increasing dt_snap if T_prod/dt_snap would exceed this cap, and to record the actual dt_snap used in sim_params.json.

- Step 2 (Data quality): The instruction to compute VACF at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps should explicitly state that the VACF must be normalized by C_vv(0) (the zero-lag variance of tracer velocities), and that the decorrelation time τ_corr should be reported in both steps and physical time units. This normalization detail is critical for comparing with the threshold C_vv(τ=1) > 0.3.

- Step 3 (Non-stationarity analysis): The McCulloch quantile method instruction references interpolating from the "published McCulloch (1986) table" but does not specify what to do when the computed ν_α falls outside the table's valid range. The engineer should be instructed to clamp ν_α to the table's valid range [1.0, 2.0] and flag any windows where clamping occurs, as these indicate near-Gaussian behavior or numerical issues.

- Step 3 (Non-stationarity analysis): The instruction to fit VACF algebraic decay to get ν(t) does not specify the fitting range. The engineer should be instructed to fit only over lags where C_vv(τ) is between 0.05 and 0.8 (excluding the noisy tail and the near-zero-lag plateau), and to report the R² of the fit to assess reliability.

- Step 5 (Wavelet decomposition): The instruction to re-advect tracers using "saved tracer positions as seeds" is ambiguous — it should clarify that the initial positions for re-advection are the tracer positions at the START of the production run (i.e., the first snapshot of tracer_positions.npy), not positions at intermediate times. This ensures all filtered-field re-advections are comparable and start from the same initial condition.

- Step 5 (Wavelet decomposition): The temporal autocorrelation C_E(τ,j) computation requires the velocity snapshots to be densely sampled in time. Since velocity snapshots are saved at dt_vel=0.5, the engineer should be instructed to verify that the number of velocity snapshots is sufficient to resolve T_eddy(j) for all levels (i.e., T_eddy(j) >> dt_vel for all j), and to print a warning if T_eddy(j) < 5×dt_vel for any level, as this would make the T_eddy estimate unreliable.

- Step 7 (Synthesis): The mapping from k_peak(t) to dominant wavelet scale j_peak(t) using k_j ≈ 128/2^j should be made more precise — the engineer should be instructed to use the actual characteristic wavenumber of each wavelet level computed from the DWT filter bank (not just the approximation 128/2^j), and to print the mapping table of j → k_j for verification.

- Step 9 (Results section): The researcher should be explicitly instructed to include a dedicated paragraph on the limitations of the analysis — specifically addressing the reduced resolution (N=512 vs. the problem statement's mandatory N=1024), the impact of the 128×128 coarsening on wavelet decomposition fidelity, and whether the 7200-second timeout constraint may have forced early termination of the DNS, potentially affecting the stationarity conclusions.

name: reviewer_response_evaluator
content:
**Recommendations:**

Step 1 (DNS simulation): The instruction to coarsen velocity/vorticity fields from 512→128 during the simulation conflicts with the problem statement's mandatory output shapes specifying 256×256 coarsened fields. The engineer should be explicitly instructed to coarsen to 128×128 (consistent with the N=512 plan deviation) AND to document this discrepancy from the problem statement in sim_params.json, so that downstream steps (especially Step 5 wavelet decomposition) load the correct 128×128 fields rather than expecting 256×256.

Step 1 (DNS simulation): The instruction to save energy_spectrum.npy with shape (N_tracer_snaps, N//2) is ambiguous when N=512 — this means 256 wavenumber bins. The engineer should be told to save the azimuthally-averaged spectrum at every tracer snapshot time (not just at Eulerian snapshot times), and to explicitly print the shape of energy_spectrum.npy after saving so downstream steps can verify the array dimensions before loading.

Step 1 (DNS simulation): The production run duration T_prod = 50×T_L could result in an extremely large number of tracer snapshots if T_L is large (e.g., T_L=10 → T_prod=500 → 50,000 snapshots at dt_snap=0.01). The engineer should be instructed to cap N_tracer_snaps at a maximum of 10,000 by adaptively increasing dt_snap if T_prod/dt_snap would exceed this cap, and to record the actual dt_snap used in sim_params.json.

Step 2 (Data quality): The instruction to compute VACF at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps should explicitly state that the VACF must be normalized by C_vv(0) (the zero-lag variance of tracer velocities), and that the decorrelation time τ_corr should be reported in both steps and physical time units. This normalization detail is critical for comparing with the threshold C_vv(τ=1) > 0.3.

Step 3 (Non-stationarity analysis): The McCulloch quantile method instruction references interpolating from the published McCulloch (1986) table but does not specify what to do when the computed ν_α falls outside the table's valid range. The engineer should be instructed to clamp ν_α to the table's valid range [1.0, 2.0] and flag any windows where clamping occurs, as these indicate near-Gaussian behavior or numerical issues.

Step 3 (Non-stationarity analysis): The instruction to fit VACF algebraic decay to get ν(t) does not specify the fitting range. The engineer should be instructed to fit only over lags where C_vv(τ) is between 0.05 and 0.8 (excluding the noisy tail and the near-zero-lag plateau), and to report the R² of the fit to assess reliability.

Step 5 (Wavelet decomposition): The instruction to re-advect tracers using saved tracer positions as seeds is ambiguous — it should clarify that the initial positions for re-advection are the tracer positions at the START of the production run (i.e., the first snapshot of tracer_positions.npy), not positions at intermediate times. This ensures all filtered-field re-advections are comparable and start from the same initial condition.

Step 5 (Wavelet decomposition): The temporal autocorrelation C_E(τ,j) computation requires the velocity snapshots to be densely sampled in time. Since velocity snapshots are saved at dt_vel=0.5, the engineer should be instructed to verify that the number of velocity snapshots is sufficient to resolve T_eddy(j) for all levels (i.e., T_eddy(j) >> dt_vel for all j), and to print a warning if T_eddy(j) < 5×dt_vel for any level, as this would make the T_eddy estimate unreliable.

Step 7 (Synthesis): The mapping from k_peak(t) to dominant wavelet scale j_peak(t) using k_j ≈ 128/2^j should be made more precise — the engineer should be instructed to use the actual characteristic wavenumber of each wavelet level computed from the DWT filter bank (not just the approximation 128/2^j), and to print the mapping table of j → k_j for verification.

Step 9 (Results section): The researcher should be explicitly instructed to include a dedicated paragraph on the limitations of the analysis — specifically addressing the reduced resolution (N=512 vs. the problem statement's mandatory N=1024), the impact of the 128×128 coarsening on wavelet decomposition fidelity, and whether the 7200-second timeout constraint may have forced early termination of the DNS, potentially affecting the stationarity conclusions.

name: review_recorder
content:
Recommendations have been logged.
Number of feedback rounds left: 0.
Now, update the plan accordingly, planner!

name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: DNS simulation — 2D Navier-Stokes with inverse cascade, tracer advection, and data output
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Implement GPU-accelerated pseudo-spectral 2D NS solver in vorticity-streamfunction form using PyTorch (device='cuda') with torch.fft.rfft2/irfft2
        - Use N=512 grid, domain [0,2π]², forcing at k∈[10,12], hyperviscosity ν_h=1e-28, p=4; use integrating factor method for hyperviscous term (mandatory — do NOT use explicit RK4 for this term)
        - Calibrate force_amp empirically: inject with force_amp=1 for 20 steps at dt=0.1, measure dE/dt per unit amp², set force_amp=sqrt(epsilon_inj/dE_per_amp2); expected ~80,000–100,000
        - Spinup: run until T_spinup=20.0, monitoring k_peak; save diagnostics only during spinup (no tracer or velocity snapshots)
        - At end of spinup, compute T_L = 2π/U_rms where U_rms=sqrt(2*E_total); seed N_tracers=5000 uniformly; verify U_rms*dt_snap < 0.1, T_L > 0, k_peak ≥ 2 before proceeding
        - Cap N_tracer_snaps at 10,000: compute dt_snap_actual = max(0.01, T_prod/10000) and use this as the actual tracer snapshot interval; record dt_snap_actual in sim_params.json
        - Production: run for T_prod=50×T_L, saving tracer positions+velocities at dt_snap_actual; save Eulerian velocity/vorticity coarsened 512→128 (NOT 256) at dt_vel=0.5
        - All coarsened Eulerian fields are 128×128 (not 256×256 as in the original problem statement); document this deviation in sim_params.json with key "coarsened_resolution": 128
        - Save energy_spectrum.npy at every tracer snapshot time with shape (N_tracer_snaps, 256) — 256 wavenumber bins for N=512; print the shape after saving for downstream verification
        - Save diagnostics.npy as structured array with fields (time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate) at every tracer snapshot time
        - Save sim_params.json with all key parameters including N, dt_snap_actual, dt_vel, nu_h, epsilon_inj, N_tracers, N_tracer_snaps, N_vel_snaps, T_spinup, T_prod, T_L_at_start_of_production, coarsened_resolution, force_amp_calibrated
        - Print key diagnostics (time, E_total, k_peak, U_rms, T_L) every 50 tracer snapshot intervals during production
      * code_execution_timeout: 7200

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: Data quality verification and inverse cascade validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy from the data directory; load sim_params.json to get actual dt_snap and coarsened_resolution
        - Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot ⟨|Δr(1 step)|⟩ using periodic-boundary-aware wrapping (wrap differences into [-π, π]); verify result is < 0.3×2π ≈ 1.88; print result and explicitly flag if exceeded
        - Compute VACF C_vv(τ) normalized by C_vv(0) (zero-lag velocity variance) at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; verify normalized C_vv(τ=1) > 0.3; report decorrelation time τ_corr (first lag where normalized C_vv drops below 1/e) in both steps and physical time units
        - Confirm inverse cascade: extract E(k,t) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod from energy_spectrum.npy; identify k_peak at each time fraction; verify drift from ~10–12 toward lower k
        - Generate a single figure with 3 subplots: (1) mean displacement per snapshot vs time, (2) normalized VACF vs lag in steps and physical time, (3) E(k) at 4 time fractions on log-log axes showing cascade evolution with k_peak marked
        - Print all key numerical results: mean displacement value, normalized C_vv at each lag, τ_corr in steps and physical time, k_peak at each time fraction

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: Non-stationarity analysis — α(t), γ(t), ν(t), k_peak(t) across overlapping time windows
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy, sim_params.json
        - Define overlapping windows of width W=8 T_L, step ΔW=2 T_L; compute and print number of windows
        - For each window: compute displacement PDFs at lags τ={0.5,1,2}×T_L; fit α via McCulloch quantile method using ν_α=(q95-q5)/(q75-q25); clamp ν_α to valid range [1.0, 2.0] and flag windows where clamping occurs; also compute Hill estimator (top-k order statistics) as cross-check
        - For each window: check CCDF log-log linearity over ≥1.5 decades; flag non-power-law windows
        - For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law over available lags to get γ(t); fit VACF algebraic decay only over lags where normalized C_vv is between 0.05 and 0.8 to get ν(t); report R² of VACF fit; extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics
        - Perform ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on the α(t) time series; print test statistics and p-values
        - Fit α = a + b×(k_peak/k_box)^c using nonlinear least squares; print fitted parameters a, b, c with uncertainties
        - Save all window results to npz file: α_mcculloch, α_hill, γ, ν, ν_r2, d_vv, k_peak, window_centers, flagged_clamped, flagged_nonpowerlaw, adf_stat, kpss_stat, fit_params

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Plot non-stationarity analysis results
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load saved window results npz from Step 3 and sim_params.json
        - Generate a multi-panel figure (3×2 layout) showing: α_mcculloch(t) and α_hill(t) vs time with flagged windows marked as open symbols and ADF/KPSS test results as text annotation; γ(t) vs time; ν(t) vs time with R² values; k_peak(t) vs time; d_vv(t) vs time; α vs k_peak/k_box scatter with fitted curve α=a+b×(k_peak/k_box)^c overlaid
        - Use physical time units on all time axes; label all axes with units
        - Print all fitted parameters and stationarity test statistics to console

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Wavelet decomposition, eddy-lifetime estimation, and filtered tracer re-advection
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape: N_vel_snaps × 2 × 128 × 128), vel_times.npy, tracer_positions.npy, and sim_params.json; confirm coarsened_resolution=128
        - Apply 2D DWT using db4 wavelet with periodic boundary mode to each velocity snapshot at levels j=1..5; extract detail coefficients D_j^{H,V,D} at each level
        - Reconstruct filtered velocity fields ũ_j for j_min∈{1,2,3,4} by zeroing detail coefficients below j_min
        - Compute wavelet energy E_j(x,y,t) = Σ_d |D_j^d(x,y,t)|² for each level j and snapshot t; compute temporal autocorrelation C_E(τ,j) averaged over spatial positions; integrate until C_E < 0.1 to get T_eddy(j); print warning if T_eddy(j) < 5×dt_vel for any level (unreliable estimate)
        - Compute and print the actual characteristic wavenumber k_j for each wavelet level j from the DWT filter bank center frequencies (not just the approximation 128/2^j); print the mapping table j → k_j
        - For each filtered field ũ_j: re-advect 5000 tracers starting from the tracer positions at the FIRST snapshot of tracer_positions.npy (start of production run); use RK4 integration with bilinear interpolation of filtered velocity on GPU (device=cuda); run for the same duration as the production run using the filtered velocity time series
        - Compute α(j) via McCulloch method (with clamping to [1,2]), γ_MSD(j) from MSD power law, ν_VACF(j) from VACF decay for each filtered field
        - Save all wavelet results to npz: T_eddy per level, k_j per level, α_j, γ_j, ν_j, and any reliability flags
      * code_execution_timeout: 7200

- Step 6:
      * sub-task: Plot wavelet scaling results and CTRW prediction test
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load wavelet results npz from Step 5
        - Generate multi-panel figure (2×2): (1) α(j) vs T_eddy(j) with fitted curve and CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β) overlaid; (2) γ_MSD(j) vs wavelet level j; (3) ν_VACF(j) vs wavelet level j; (4) T_eddy(j) vs wavelet level j on log scale
        - Fit β(j) ∝ T_eddy(j)^δ and report δ; compute predicted α from CTRW formula α=1+β/(2-β) and overlay on panel (1)
        - Print all fitted parameters: δ, functional form of α vs T_eddy, β values per level, predicted vs observed α per level

- Step 7:
      * sub-task: Synthesis — compare α(t) vs α(j), 2D map, stationarity conclusion
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load window results npz from Step 3 and wavelet results npz from Step 5
        - Use the printed k_j mapping table from Step 5 to map k_peak(t) to dominant wavelet scale j_peak(t): for each window, find j that minimizes |k_j - k_peak(t)|; print the resulting j_peak(t) series
        - Compare α(t) from Step 3 with α(j_peak(t)) from Step 5 on a single panel; compute and print Pearson correlation coefficient and RMSE
        - Construct 2D scatter map of α as function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using all available data points from both window analysis and wavelet levels; fit a surface or report trends
        - Save synthesis results to npz: alpha_t, alpha_j_peak, j_peak_series, correlation, rmse, T_eddy_grid, kpeak_grid, alpha_2d

- Step 8:
      * sub-task: Plot synthesis results
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load synthesis results npz from Step 7
        - Generate a figure with 2 panels: (1) α(t) and α(j_peak(t)) vs time on same axes with legend, correlation and RMSE annotated; (2) 2D scatter/contour map of α as function of (T_eddy on x-axis, k_peak/k_box on y-axis) with color encoding α values and colorbar
        - Print correlation coefficient, RMSE, and key synthesis numbers to console

- Step 9:
      * sub-task: Write full Results section of the paper
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Write a complete Results section in academic paper style with four subsections matching the analysis tasks: (1) Simulation Quality and Inverse Cascade Validation, (2) Non-Stationary Lévy Analysis, (3) Wavelet-Filtered Eddy Lifetime Scaling, (4) Synthesis
        - In subsection 1: report mean displacement per snapshot, normalized VACF values at each lag, τ_corr in physical units, k_peak evolution across T_prod, and whether quality thresholds were met
        - In subsection 2: report α(t) values (mean, range, trend), γ(t), ν(t) with R² values, ADF and KPSS test statistics and p-values, fitted parameters a/b/c for α vs k_peak/k_box, number of flagged windows, comparison of McCulloch vs Hill estimators
        - In subsection 3: report T_eddy(j) for each wavelet level, k_j mapping, α(j) values, γ_MSD(j), ν_VACF(j), fitted δ for β∝T_eddy^δ, CTRW prediction quality (predicted vs observed α), any reliability warnings
        - In subsection 4: report correlation between α(t) and α(j_peak(t)), RMSE, description of 2D α map trends, and overall conclusion on stationarity of α
        - Include a dedicated Limitations paragraph addressing: N=512 vs mandatory N=1024, impact of 128×128 coarsening on wavelet fidelity, potential early termination due to 7200s timeout, and how these affect stationarity conclusions
        - Discuss whether α≈1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts with spectral condensation, and whether CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β) with β∝T_eddy^δ is supported by the data
        - Reference all generated plots by their panel descriptions

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:
**PLAN**

- Step 1:
	* sub-task: DNS simulation — 2D Navier-Stokes with inverse cascade, tracer advection, and data output
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 7200s

	* instructions:
		- Implement GPU-accelerated pseudo-spectral 2D NS solver in vorticity-streamfunction form using PyTorch (device='cuda') with torch.fft.rfft2/irfft2
		- Use N=512 grid, domain [0,2π]², forcing at k∈[10,12], hyperviscosity ν_h=1e-28, p=4; use integrating factor method for hyperviscous term (mandatory — do NOT use explicit RK4 for this term)
		- Calibrate force_amp empirically: inject with force_amp=1 for 20 steps at dt=0.1, measure dE/dt per unit amp², set force_amp=sqrt(epsilon_inj/dE_per_amp2); expected ~80,000–100,000
		- Spinup: run until T_spinup=20.0, monitoring k_peak; save diagnostics only during spinup (no tracer or velocity snapshots)
		- At end of spinup, compute T_L = 2π/U_rms where U_rms=sqrt(2*E_total); seed N_tracers=5000 uniformly; verify U_rms*dt_snap < 0.1, T_L > 0, k_peak ≥ 2 before proceeding
		- Cap N_tracer_snaps at 10,000: compute dt_snap_actual = max(0.01, T_prod/10000) and use this as the actual tracer snapshot interval; record dt_snap_actual in sim_params.json
		- Production: run for T_prod=50×T_L, saving tracer positions+velocities at dt_snap_actual; save Eulerian velocity/vorticity coarsened 512→128 (NOT 256) at dt_vel=0.5
		- All coarsened Eulerian fields are 128×128 (not 256×256 as in the original problem statement); document this deviation in sim_params.json with key "coarsened_resolution": 128
		- Save energy_spectrum.npy at every tracer snapshot time with shape (N_tracer_snaps, 256) — 256 wavenumber bins for N=512; print the shape after saving for downstream verification
		- Save diagnostics.npy as structured array with fields (time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate) at every tracer snapshot time
		- Save sim_params.json with all key parameters including N, dt_snap_actual, dt_vel, nu_h, epsilon_inj, N_tracers, N_tracer_snaps, N_vel_snaps, T_spinup, T_prod, T_L_at_start_of_production, coarsened_resolution, force_amp_calibrated
		- Print key diagnostics (time, E_total, k_peak, U_rms, T_L) every 50 tracer snapshot intervals during production

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: Data quality verification and inverse cascade validation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy from the data directory; load sim_params.json to get actual dt_snap and coarsened_resolution
		- Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot ⟨|Δr(1 step)|⟩ using periodic-boundary-aware wrapping (wrap differences into [-π, π]); verify result is < 0.3×2π ≈ 1.88; print result and explicitly flag if exceeded
		- Compute VACF C_vv(τ) normalized by C_vv(0) (zero-lag velocity variance) at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; verify normalized C_vv(τ=1) > 0.3; report decorrelation time τ_corr (first lag where normalized C_vv drops below 1/e) in both steps and physical time units
		- Confirm inverse cascade: extract E(k,t) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod from energy_spectrum.npy; identify k_peak at each time fraction; verify drift from ~10–12 toward lower k
		- Generate a single figure with 3 subplots: (1) mean displacement per snapshot vs time, (2) normalized VACF vs lag in steps and physical time, (3) E(k) at 4 time fractions on log-log axes showing cascade evolution with k_peak marked
		- Print all key numerical results: mean displacement value, normalized C_vv at each lag, τ_corr in steps and physical time, k_peak at each time fraction

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Non-stationarity analysis — α(t), γ(t), ν(t), k_peak(t) across overlapping time windows
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy, sim_params.json
		- Define overlapping windows of width W=8 T_L, step ΔW=2 T_L; compute and print number of windows
		- For each window: compute displacement PDFs at lags τ={0.5,1,2}×T_L; fit α via McCulloch quantile method using ν_α=(q95-q5)/(q75-q25); clamp ν_α to valid range [1.0, 2.0] and flag windows where clamping occurs; also compute Hill estimator (top-k order statistics) as cross-check
		- For each window: check CCDF log-log linearity over ≥1.5 decades; flag non-power-law windows
		- For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law over available lags to get γ(t); fit VACF algebraic decay only over lags where normalized C_vv is between 0.05 and 0.8 to get ν(t); report R² of VACF fit; extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics
		- Perform ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on the α(t) time series; print test statistics and p-values
		- Fit α = a + b×(k_peak/k_box)^c using nonlinear least squares; print fitted parameters a, b, c with uncertainties
		- Save all window results to npz file: α_mcculloch, α_hill, γ, ν, ν_r2, d_vv, k_peak, window_centers, flagged_clamped, flagged_nonpowerlaw, adf_stat, kpss_stat, fit_params

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Plot non-stationarity analysis results
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load saved window results npz from Step 3 and sim_params.json
		- Generate a multi-panel figure (3×2 layout) showing: α_mcculloch(t) and α_hill(t) vs time with flagged windows marked as open symbols and ADF/KPSS test results as text annotation; γ(t) vs time; ν(t) vs time with R² values; k_peak(t) vs time; d_vv(t) vs time; α vs k_peak/k_box scatter with fitted curve α=a+b×(k_peak/k_box)^c overlaid
		- Use physical time units on all time axes; label all axes with units
		- Print all fitted parameters and stationarity test statistics to console

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Wavelet decomposition, eddy-lifetime estimation, and filtered tracer re-advection
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 7200s

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape: N_vel_snaps × 2 × 128 × 128), vel_times.npy, tracer_positions.npy, and sim_params.json; confirm coarsened_resolution=128
		- Apply 2D DWT using db4 wavelet with periodic boundary mode to each velocity snapshot at levels j=1..5; extract detail coefficients D_j^{H,V,D} at each level
		- Reconstruct filtered velocity fields ũ_j for j_min∈{1,2,3,4} by zeroing detail coefficients below j_min
		- Compute wavelet energy E_j(x,y,t) = Σ_d |D_j^d(x,y,t)|² for each level j and snapshot t; compute temporal autocorrelation C_E(τ,j) averaged over spatial positions; integrate until C_E < 0.1 to get T_eddy(j); print warning if T_eddy(j) < 5×dt_vel for any level (unreliable estimate)
		- Compute and print the actual characteristic wavenumber k_j for each wavelet level j from the DWT filter bank center frequencies (not just the approximation 128/2^j); print the mapping table j → k_j
		- For each filtered field ũ_j: re-advect 5000 tracers starting from the tracer positions at the FIRST snapshot of tracer_positions.npy (start of production run); use RK4 integration with bilinear interpolation of filtered velocity on GPU (device=cuda); run for the same duration as the production run using the filtered velocity time series
		- Compute α(j) via McCulloch method (with clamping to [1,2]), γ_MSD(j) from MSD power law, ν_VACF(j) from VACF decay for each filtered field
		- Save all wavelet results to npz: T_eddy per level, k_j per level, α_j, γ_j, ν_j, and any reliability flags

- Step 6:
	* sub-task: Plot wavelet scaling results and CTRW prediction test
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load wavelet results npz from Step 5
		- Generate multi-panel figure (2×2): (1) α(j) vs T_eddy(j) with fitted curve and CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β) overlaid; (2) γ_MSD(j) vs wavelet level j; (3) ν_VACF(j) vs wavelet level j; (4) T_eddy(j) vs wavelet level j on log scale
		- Fit β(j) ∝ T_eddy(j)^δ and report δ; compute predicted α from CTRW formula α=1+β/(2-β) and overlay on panel (1)
		- Print all fitted parameters: δ, functional form of α vs T_eddy, β values per level, predicted vs observed α per level

- Step 7:
	* sub-task: Synthesis — compare α(t) vs α(j), 2D map, stationarity conclusion
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load window results npz from Step 3 and wavelet results npz from Step 5
		- Use the printed k_j mapping table from Step 5 to map k_peak(t) to dominant wavelet scale j_peak(t): for each window, find j that minimizes |k_j - k_peak(t)|; print the resulting j_peak(t) series
		- Compare α(t) from Step 3 with α(j_peak(t)) from Step 5 on a single panel; compute and print Pearson correlation coefficient and RMSE
		- Construct 2D scatter map of α as function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using all available data points from both window analysis and wavelet levels; fit a surface or report trends
		- Save synthesis results to npz: alpha_t, alpha_j_peak, j_peak_series, correlation, rmse, T_eddy_grid, kpeak_grid, alpha_2d

- Step 8:
	* sub-task: Plot synthesis results
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load synthesis results npz from Step 7
		- Generate a figure with 2 panels: (1) α(t) and α(j_peak(t)) vs time on same axes with legend, correlation and RMSE annotated; (2) 2D scatter/contour map of α as function of (T_eddy on x-axis, k_peak/k_box on y-axis) with color encoding α values and colorbar
		- Print correlation coefficient, RMSE, and key synthesis numbers to console

- Step 9:
	* sub-task: Write full Results section of the paper
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Write a complete Results section in academic paper style with four subsections matching the analysis tasks: (1) Simulation Quality and Inverse Cascade Validation, (2) Non-Stationary Lévy Analysis, (3) Wavelet-Filtered Eddy Lifetime Scaling, (4) Synthesis
		- In subsection 1: report mean displacement per snapshot, normalized VACF values at each lag, τ_corr in physical units, k_peak evolution across T_prod, and whether quality thresholds were met
		- In subsection 2: report α(t) values (mean, range, trend), γ(t), ν(t) with R² values, ADF and KPSS test statistics and p-values, fitted parameters a/b/c for α vs k_peak/k_box, number of flagged windows, comparison of McCulloch vs Hill estimators
		- In subsection 3: report T_eddy(j) for each wavelet level, k_j mapping, α(j) values, γ_MSD(j), ν_VACF(j), fitted δ for β∝T_eddy^δ, CTRW prediction quality (predicted vs observed α), any reliability warnings
		- In subsection 4: report correlation between α(t) and α(j_peak(t)), RMSE, description of 2D α map trends, and overall conclusion on stationarity of α
		- Include a dedicated Limitations paragraph addressing: N=512 vs mandatory N=1024, impact of 128×128 coarsening on wavelet fidelity, potential early termination due to 7200s timeout, and how these affect stationarity conclusions
		- Discuss whether α≈1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts with spectral condensation, and whether CTRW prediction α=1+β/(2-β) with β∝T_eddy^δ is supported by the data
		- Reference all generated plots by their panel descriptions

name: plan_recorder
content: Plan recorded as final. Planning stage complete.
